test_cases.title: Async export initiated for dataset exceeding 50,000 rows returns immediate acknowledgment with job ID
preconditions: User has no active export jobs
A dataset with more than 50,000 rows is available and accessible to the user
steps: Send an export request for a dataset containing 50,001 rows
test_cases.expected_result: The system returns an immediate acknowledgment response (e.g., HTTP 202 Accepted) containing a unique job ID, without waiting for the export to complete.

test_cases.title: Export request for dataset at or below 50,000 rows is NOT processed asynchronously
preconditions: A dataset with exactly 50,000 rows is available and accessible to the user
steps: Send an export request for a dataset containing exactly 50,000 rows
test_cases.expected_result: The system does not initiate an asynchronous job for this request; the export is handled synchronously or the response does not include an async job ID.

test_cases.title: User receives email and in-app notification when async export job completes
preconditions: User is authenticated and has a verified email address
An async export job has been successfully initiated for a dataset larger than 50,000 rows
The export job ID is known
steps: Wait for the export job to reach a completed state
Check the user's registered email inbox for an export-ready notification
Check the user's in-app notification center for an export-ready notification
test_cases.expected_result: The user receives both an email notification and an in-app notification indicating the export is ready, each containing or linking to the download.

test_cases.title: Download link provided in export notification expires after 48 hours
preconditions: An async export job has completed
The user has received the download link
steps: Record the exact timestamp when the download link was generated
Access the download link immediately after generation and confirm it is valid
Advance system or test time to 48 hours and 1 minute after link generation
Attempt to access the same download link again
test_cases.expected_result: The download link is accessible before the 48-hour window expires and returns an error (e.g., HTTP 410 Gone or HTTP 403 Forbidden) after 48 hours have elapsed.

test_cases.title: Second concurrent export request for the same user is rejected while an active export job exists
preconditions: User already has one active (in-progress) export job for a dataset larger than 50,000 rows
steps: Send a second export request for any dataset larger than 50,000 rows using the same user account
test_cases.expected_result: The system rejects the second export request with an appropriate error response (e.g., HTTP 409 Conflict) indicating the user already has an active export job.

test_cases.title: Unauthenticated user cannot initiate an async export request
preconditions: No valid authentication token or session exists
steps: Send an export request for a dataset larger than 50,000 rows without any authentication credentials
test_cases.expected_result: The system rejects the request with an authentication error (e.g., HTTP 401 Unauthorized) and does not create any export job.

assumptions: The 50,000-row threshold is exclusive (i.e., async processing applies to datasets with strictly more than 50,000 rows, not exactly 50,000).
The 48-hour validity window begins at the moment the download link is generated, not when the notification is sent.
An 'active' export job means any job in a pending or in-progress state; completed, failed, or cancelled jobs do not count toward the concurrent limit.
The user's email address is already verified and on file; no test cases are required for unverified email scenarios.
The system provides a way (e.g., a status endpoint or UI indicator) to confirm a job is in an active state, allowing preconditions for concurrency tests to be established.
A distinct error response or code is returned when the concurrent export limit is reached, distinguishable from other error types.
notes: The requirement does not specify: (1) what happens if the export job fails — whether the user is notified and through which channels; (2) whether the concurrent limit of one applies across all dataset export types or only large async exports; (3) the exact format or content required in the email and in-app notifications; (4) whether a user can cancel an active export job to free up the concurrent slot; (5) what happens to the download link if the underlying exported file is deleted before 48 hours elapse; (6) any rate limiting or queue behavior if the user repeatedly submits new export requests after each one completes. These gaps may require additional test cases once clarified.
